topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my afternoon. My stomach tightened instantly, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my breath catching in my throat as I read the headline. My name wasn't there. Instead, a stranger's name was front and center, attached to the article I had poured weeks of meticulous research and genuine passion into. A slow, creeping sense of wrongness began to coil in my chest. I reread the byline, the unfamiliar flourish of the author's signature feeling like a physical slap. I started pacing the small apartment, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. Every time I looked at the screen, a prickle of apprehension danced across my skin. Who was this person? And why?

[story 2]

I stared at the printout on my desk, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh office light. The words were mine—my analysis, my unique perspective—but the name printed beneath them belonged to someone else entirely. A tremor ran through my hand as I picked up the paper, the edges feeling suddenly brittle. I traced the unfamiliar script with my fingertip, the texture of the paper mocking my sense of ownership. A strange hollowness settled in my chest, a sudden lack of buoyancy. I tried to rationalize it, searching for a loophole, a simple clerical error, but the discrepancy felt too deliberate, too pointed. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and just sat there, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 3]

When the email arrived, the silence in the room became suffocating. I read the attribution, and the color seemed to drain from the world around me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. It wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a calculated erasure. I pushed the laptop away, the sudden movement jarring. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to peer out at the busy street below, but the motion felt clumsy, disconnected. My palms grew slick. I kept replaying the details—the specific phrasing, the nuanced arguments—and the realization that someone else was claiming my intellectual labor sent a wave of profound disorientation through me. I needed to verify, to confront this, but the energy required to move felt immense, paralyzing me to the spot.
